---
title: "Understanding File Paths"
subtitle: "Navigate your computer like a pro and troubleshoot 'file not found' errors with confidence."
author: "Curt Rode"
date: last-modified
difficulty: beginner
time-estimate: "15 min"
format:
  html:
    code-tools: false
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
## Learning Objectives
By the end of this guide, you will be able to:

- Understand absolute vs. relative file paths
- Navigate using command line path syntax
- Troubleshoot common path-related errors
- Use paths effectively in Python and VS Code
:::

File paths are like addresses for your files—they tell your computer exactly where to find something. Understanding paths is crucial for working with Python scripts, VS Code projects, and any command-line tools in digital humanities.

## What Are File Paths?

A file path is the complete location of a file or folder on your computer. Think of it like a postal address that gets more specific as you read from left to right.

```{=html}
<div class="path-example">
  <h4>🏠 Path as Address Analogy</h4>
  <div class="analogy-comparison">
    <div class="postal-address">
      <strong>Postal Address:</strong><br>
      123 Main Street<br>
      Springfield, IL 62704<br>
      United States
    </div>
    <div class="file-path">
      <strong>File Path:</strong><br>
      <code>/Users/student/Documents/DH-Projects/research-notes.txt</code>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

## Absolute vs. Relative Paths

**Absolute path** = full location from the root of the computer.

**Mac Examples:**
```
/Users/sarah/Documents/DH-Projects/victorian-novels/data.csv
/Applications/Visual Studio Code.app
/System/Library/Fonts/Arial.ttf
```

**PC Examples:**
```
C:\Users\Sarah\Documents\DH-Projects\victorian-novels\data.csv
C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\Code.exe
D:\External Drive\Backup\research-archive.zip
```

**Relative path** = location relative to where you are right now.

```{=html}
<div class="quiz" data-quiz="path-types">
  <h3>Quick Check: Absolute vs Relative</h3>
  <p>Which of these is a relative path?</p>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="path-types" value="a">
    <code>/home/student/thesis.txt</code>
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option" data-correct>
    <input type="radio" name="path-types" value="b">
    <code>../images/manuscript.jpg</code>
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="path-types" value="c">
    <code>C:\Users\John\Desktop\script.py</code>
  </label>
  <div class="feedback-correct">
    <p><strong>Correct!</strong> <code>../images/manuscript.jpg</code> is relative because it depends on your current folder.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="feedback-incorrect">
    <p>Absolute paths start from the root (like <code>/</code> or <code>C:\</code>). Relative paths start from where you are.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

## Platform Differences

### Mac and Linux Paths
- Start with `/` (forward slash)
- Use `/` to separate folders
- Case-sensitive (usually)
- Root directory is `/`

### Windows Paths
- Start with drive letter (`C:`, `D:`)
- Use `\` (backslash) to separate folders
- Case-insensitive
- Root directory is drive letter (`C:\`)

```{=html}
<div class="quiz" data-quiz="platform-paths">
  <h3>Quick Check: Platform Recognition</h3>
  <p>Which path belongs to a Windows computer?</p>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="platform-quiz" value="mac">
    <code>/Users/student/Documents/thesis.docx</code>
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option" data-correct>
    <input type="radio" name="platform-quiz" value="windows">
    <code>C:\Users\student\Documents\thesis.docx</code>
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="platform-quiz" value="linux">
    <code>/home/student/Documents/thesis.docx</code>
  </label>
  <div class="feedback-correct">
    <p><strong>Exactly!</strong> Windows paths start with a drive letter and use backslashes.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="feedback-incorrect">
    <p>Look for a drive letter like <code>C:\</code> and backslashes.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

## Special Path Symbols

- `.` = current directory
- `..` = parent directory
- `~` = home directory (Mac/Linux)
- `%USERPROFILE%` = home directory (Windows)

## Common Errors (and Fixes)

- **"File not found"** → check spelling and working directory.
- **Spaces in path** → wrap in quotes, or escape spaces.
- **Permissions** → check read/write permissions.

## Hands-On Practice

### Exercise 1: Path Translation

Given this structure:
```
/Users/alex/DH-Research/
├── 01-data/
│   ├── raw/
│   │   └── survey-responses.csv
│   └── processed/
│       └── clean-data.csv
├── 02-scripts/
│   ├── clean-data.py
│   └── analyze.py
└── 03-outputs/
    └── results.txt
```

Try these, then check answers:
1) From `02-scripts/`, write a relative path to `survey-responses.csv`.
2) Write the absolute path to `clean-data.py`.
3) From `01-data/processed/`, write a relative path to `results.txt`.

<details>
<summary>Show answers</summary>

1) `../01-data/raw/survey-responses.csv`

2) `/Users/alex/DH-Research/02-scripts/clean-data.py`

3) `../../03-outputs/results.txt`
</details>

### Exercise 2: Fix the Python Script

Broken code:
```python
import sys
sys.path.append('helpers.py')  # Problem 1
from helpers import clean_text

# Problem 2
with open('novels.txt', 'r') as f:
    text = f.read()

cleaned = clean_text(text)
print(cleaned)
```

<details>
<summary>Show a corrected version</summary>

```python
import sys
from pathlib import Path

project_root = Path(__file__).parent
sys.path.append(str(project_root / 'utils'))

from helpers import clean_text

with open(project_root / 'data' / 'novels.txt', 'r') as f:
    text = f.read()

cleaned = clean_text(text)
print(cleaned)
```
</details>

## Path Libraries in Python

```python
from pathlib import Path

project_root = Path.home() / 'DH-Research'
data_file = project_root / 'data' / 'texts.csv'

if data_file.exists():
    print(f"Found file: {data_file}")
```
